title: How the Digital World Actually Works

required.title: [For Student] Tubes: A Journey to the Center of the Internet
required.author: Andrew Blum

required.title: [For Mentor] The Age of Surveillance Capitalism
required.author: Shoshana Zuboff

suggested.title: [For Student] Wireshark or similar packet-capture tool

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach
supplementary.author: Kurose and Ross

narration_prompts.detail: from the moment you type a URL to the moment the page appears on your screen. Name each step: client, DNS, IP address, server, packets, response. What happens at each point?

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain the business model of a platform you use.
narration_prompts.detail: How does it make money? What is it optimizing for? How does that optimization affect what you see and how you behave on the platform?

narration_prompts.prompt: You read a real terms of service.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you find? What rights did you grant the company? What surprised you? What would most people not know about the contract they signed?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between encrypted and unencrypted communication?
narration_prompts.detail: Why does it matter? Give an example of when you would want end-to-end encryption and explain what it protects you from.

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the trade-off between storing files locally and storing them in the cloud?
narration_prompts.detail: What do you gain with each? What do you lose? How should a person decide?

cross_connections.description: Understanding of encryption, network architecture, and account structures from this packet provides the technical foundation for the security practices developed in 4.2.2-B.

cross_connections.target_name: How Algorithms Shape What You See
cross_connections.description: The economics of attention and the platform business model introduced here directly feed into 4.2.3's analysis of how algorithms curate information.

cross_connections.target_name: Informal Power, Corporate Influence & Parallel Governance
cross_connections.description: Platform power is a form of parallel governance — private companies making decisions about speech, access, and information that function as regulatory decisions without democratic accountability.

Understanding the economics of "free" services connects to financial literacy — the student who understands that they pay for free services with data and attention is making economic decisions, not just technology decisions.

Understanding the systems that deliver information is a prerequisite for evaluating the information itself. The student who knows how platforms select content is better equipped to evaluate the content selected.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned the foundations: computers are machines, the internet is physical, the cloud is someone else's computer, and someone decides what you see on screen. You can now explain what a computer is and trace the basic path of a web request.

Weathering Storms goes deeper. You are going to learn how the internet actually routes information — the client-server model, DNS, IP addressing, packets, and encryption. You are going to learn what an operating system does and why whoever controls your OS can potentially see everything you do. You are going to learn how "free" services actually make money, and you are going to read a real terms-of-service agreement and discover what you have actually agreed to.

This matters because the digital world is not optional. Your communication, your education, eventually your finances and your career will all flow through digital systems. The person who understands these systems makes informed decisions. The person who does not understand them makes decisions in the dark — about their privacy, their data, their money, and their attention. You are learning to see the system beneath the interface.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Technical understanding of internet architecture** — client-server model, DNS, IP addressing, how data travels through physical infrastructure
2. **Encryption basics** — what encryption does, what HTTPS means, why it matters
3. **Operating system awareness** — what an OS manages and why your OS choice is a sovereignty decision
4. **The economics of "free" platforms** — the attention economy, data as currency, how platforms monetize users
5. **Terms-of-service literacy** — the ability to read and interpret the contracts you sign when you click "I agree"
6. **Local versus cloud trade-offs** — understanding what you gain and lose when your data lives on someone else's hardware
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Client-Server Model — How Every Digital Interaction Works

Every time you use a website, an app, or an online service, you are participating in the client-server model. Understanding this model is understanding how the entire internet works.

**Your device is the client.** A client is any device that makes requests — your phone, your laptop, your tablet. When you open a browser and type a web address, your client is sending a request: "Please send me the contents of this page."

**The server is the computer that responds.** A server is a computer — often a powerful one sitting in a data center — that holds the website, app data, or service you are requesting. Its job is to receive requests from clients and send back responses. The word "server" literally means "one who serves." The server serves information to your client.

**Every interaction follows this pattern:**
1. Your client sends a request
2. The request travels through the network to the appropriate server
3. The server processes the request
4. The server sends a response back to your client
5. Your client displays the response

When you search Google, your client sends the search query to Google's servers. Google's servers process the query (running it through their search algorithm), assemble the results, and send them back. When you post on social media, your client sends the post to the platform's servers, which store it and then distribute it to other clients when their users check their feeds.

**Why this matters:** Everything you do online passes through someone else's server. That server is owned by a company. That company's servers see your request, process it, and can log it. When you understand the client-server model, you understand that every digital interaction has at least three participants: you, the network, and the server owner. You are never alone online.

### Part 2: DNS and IP Addresses — The Phone Book of the Internet

Every device connected to the internet has a unique address called an **IP address** — a string of numbers like 142.250.80.46. This is how computers find each other on the network. Your computer has one. Google's servers have one. Every website you visit is hosted on a server with an IP address.

But humans do not navigate the internet by typing strings of numbers. You type "google.com" or "wikipedia.org." Something has to translate the human-readable name into the machine-readable address. That something is **DNS — the Domain Name System.**

DNS is the phone book of the internet. When you type "google.com" into your browser:
1. Your computer asks a DNS server: "What is the IP address for google.com?"
2. The DNS server looks it up and responds: "142.250.80.46"
3. Your computer then sends its request to that IP address

**Why DNS matters for sovereignty:** Whoever controls DNS controls what addresses resolve — which means they control what you can access. If a government tells DNS servers within its borders to stop resolving certain domain names, those websites become unreachable for people in that country. This is how China's Great Firewall blocks access to many foreign websites. This is how governments can shut down access to specific platforms during protests. DNS is not just a technical system. It is a control point.

### Part 3: How Data Travels — Packets, Routing, and Physical Infrastructure

When you send a message or load a webpage, the data does not travel as one continuous stream. It is broken into small pieces called **packets**. Each packet contains a piece of the data plus routing information — the destination address and a sequence number so the receiving computer can reassemble the packets in the correct order.

Packets may take different routes through the network. One packet might travel through fiber-optic cables across the country. Another might take a slightly different path. They arrive at the destination, get reassembled, and you see the complete webpage or message. This system — called **packet switching** — is what makes the internet resilient. If one route is congested or broken, packets can take alternative routes.

**The physical infrastructure:**

- **Undersea cables** carry approximately 95% of intercontinental internet traffic. These are physical cables — some thinner than a garden hose — laid on the ocean floor, connecting continents. There are approximately 550 active submarine cables worldwide. If they were cut, intercontinental internet traffic would effectively stop.
- **Internet Exchange Points (IXPs)** are physical locations where different internet networks connect to each other. Major IXPs (like DE-CIX in Frankfurt or AMS-IX in Amsterdam) handle enormous amounts of traffic. They are infrastructure chokepoints — critical nodes where disruption would affect millions of users.
- **Data centers** house the servers that store and process the internet's content. The largest data centers consume as much electricity as small cities. Amazon, Google, and Microsoft operate data centers on every inhabited continent.

**The sovereignty lesson:** The internet has physical infrastructure with physical owners and physical locations. Governments, corporations, and even natural disasters can disrupt it at specific points. The internet is not an ungovernable digital realm — it is a physical system subject to physical control.

### Part 4: Encryption — Scrambling Data So Only You Can Read It

When you send data across the internet, it passes through many intermediate points — routers, switches, and other network equipment. Without encryption, anyone who controls one of those intermediate points could read your data.

**Encryption** scrambles data so that only the intended recipient can read it. Think of it as putting your message in a locked box that only the recipient has the key to open. Anyone who intercepts the box sees only scrambled nonsense.

**HTTPS — what the lock icon means:** When you see a padlock icon in your browser's address bar and the web address starts with "https://" (not "http://"), it means the connection between your browser and the server is encrypted. This means that your internet provider, the coffee shop's Wi-Fi owner, and anyone else monitoring the network can see that you are connecting to that website — but they cannot see what you are doing on it. They see the envelope, not the letter.

**What the lock icon does NOT mean:** It does not mean the website is trustworthy or safe. A phishing site — a fake website designed to steal your password — can have HTTPS and a padlock. Encryption protects the connection; it says nothing about what is on the other end.

**End-to-end encryption** is stronger. In end-to-end encryption, the data is encrypted on your device and only decrypted on the recipient's device. Not even the company running the service can read it. Signal, for example, uses end-to-end encryption for messages. This means Signal itself cannot read your messages even if ordered to by a court. Standard email, standard SMS text messages, and most social media messages are NOT end-to-end encrypted — the platform can read them.

### Part 5: Operating Systems — The Software That Sees Everything

An **operating system (OS)** is the software that manages your computer. It controls the hardware, runs applications, manages files, and mediates between you and the machine. The major operating systems are:

- **Windows** (Microsoft) — dominant on desktop and laptop computers
- **macOS** (Apple) — Apple's desktop and laptop OS
- **Linux** — open-source, community-developed, used on most servers and available for personal computers
- **iOS** (Apple) — iPhones and iPads
- **Android** (Google) — most non-Apple smartphones and tablets

**Why your OS choice matters:** Your operating system sits between you and everything you do on your device. It sees every file you open, every app you run, every website you visit, every keystroke you type. This means the company that made your OS could, in principle, see everything you do. Whether they actually collect this data depends on their policies, their settings, and your trust in their statements.

Apple and Google both collect data from their mobile operating systems — location data, app usage, device information. The extent and purpose of this collection is described in their privacy policies, which most users never read. The student who understands that their OS is a potential observation point makes different decisions about settings, defaults, and which OS to use than the student who has never considered this.

**The Linux alternative:** Linux is different because it is open source — the code is publicly available and can be inspected by anyone. No single company controls it. This does not make it perfectly secure, but it does mean that data collection cannot be hidden in the code. Linux represents a different model: transparency through openness rather than trust in a corporation.

### Part 6: The Economics of "Free" — How Platforms Actually Make Money

Google is free. YouTube is free. Instagram is free. TikTok is free. These companies are among the most valuable on Earth. How do free services generate billions of dollars?

**The answer: advertising, powered by attention and data.**

**The attention economy:** Most free platforms are funded by advertising. Advertising revenue depends on attention — the more time users spend on the platform, the more ads they see, the more money the platform makes. Therefore, the fundamental business objective of platforms like YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, and Facebook is to capture and hold your attention for as long as possible.

Every design decision serves this objective:
- **Infinite scroll** removes the natural stopping point so you keep going
- **Autoplay** starts the next video before you decide whether to watch it
- **Notifications** pull you back to the platform when you leave
- **The recommendation algorithm** learns what keeps you engaged and shows you more of it

You are not the customer of these platforms. Advertisers are the customers. You are the product — or more precisely, your attention is the product being sold to advertisers.

**Data as currency:** Platforms collect vast amounts of data about you: what you click, what you watch, how long you watch it, what you search for, where you are, who your friends are, what you buy, what you almost bought. This data is used to build a profile that predicts your behavior and preferences. That profile makes advertising more effective — and more effective advertising commands higher prices. Your data is not a byproduct of using the platform. It is the point.

**Exercise: Read a real privacy policy.** Take a platform you actually use. Find its privacy policy. Read it. Identify:
- What data does the platform collect?
- What does it do with that data?
- Who does it share the data with?
- Can you opt out of data collection and still use the service?
- Translate three sentences from the privacy policy into plain language.

### Part 7: Terms of Service — The Contract You Already Signed

Every platform has terms of service (ToS). When you created your account, you clicked "I agree" to a legal contract. Here is what is typically in that contract:

**What rights you granted the company:** Most ToS include a license granting the company broad rights to content you post — the right to use, modify, distribute, and sublicense your photos, videos, and text. This does not mean they own your content, but it means they can do many things with it.

**What the company can do with your data:** The ToS and privacy policy together describe how the company collects, uses, stores, and shares your information. The language is typically vague and permissive — "we may share your information with our partners for purposes including but not limited to..."

**When the company can terminate your access:** Most ToS give the company the right to suspend or terminate your account at their sole discretion. This means that all the content, connections, and data associated with your account exist at the pleasure of the company.

**What happens to your content if you leave:** Some platforms retain rights to your content even after you delete your account. Others delete it. The ToS specifies which.

**What legal jurisdiction governs disputes:** If you have a dispute with the platform, the ToS specifies where the case would be heard — usually the company's home jurisdiction. Many ToS include mandatory arbitration clauses, which means you cannot sue them in court.

**The exercise:** Read the terms of service for one platform you use. Identify each of these elements. Translate the most important sections into plain language a younger student could understand.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Web Request Tracing
**Developing well:** The student can trace a complete web request from URL to rendered page: client sends request, DNS resolves the name to an IP address, request travels through the physical network to the server, server processes and responds, packets travel back and are reassembled. The student names each component and understands its function.

**Developing slowly:** The student can name some components but cannot connect them into a coherent sequence. The process remains a list of vocabulary rather than an understood system.

**Diagnostic questions:** Can the student explain what would happen if DNS was unavailable? (Answer: the website name would not resolve and the site would appear unreachable, even though the server is still running.) This tests whether they understand how the pieces depend on each other.

### 2. Platform Business Model Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can articulate the business model of at least two platforms: how they make money (advertising), what they optimize for (engagement), what data they collect, and how this shapes the user experience. The student uses specific examples, not generalizations.

**Developing slowly:** The student can say "they sell ads" but cannot connect this to specific design decisions (infinite scroll, autoplay, notification systems) or to their own experience as a user.

**Diagnostic questions:** Ask: "If YouTube changed its algorithm to optimize for accuracy instead of engagement, what would change about what you see?" This tests whether the student understands the causal relationship between optimization targets and user experience.

### 3. Terms-of-Service Literacy
**Developing well:** The student has read a real ToS and can identify: rights granted, data usage policies, termination conditions, and jurisdiction. They can translate legal language into plain language. They express informed opinions about what they found.

**Developing slowly:** The student skimmed the ToS without genuine engagement. They cannot identify specific provisions or translate legal language.

**Diagnostic questions:** Ask the student to quote one specific provision from the ToS they read and explain what it means in practical terms for a user.

### 4. Encryption Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can explain what encryption does (scrambles data so only the intended recipient can read it), what HTTPS protects (the content of the connection, not the fact of the connection), and what end-to-end encryption provides (neither the company nor intermediaries can read the content).

**Developing slowly:** The student confuses encrypted with secure in general terms. They may think HTTPS means a site is trustworthy rather than that the connection is encrypted.

**Diagnostic questions:** "If a phishing site has HTTPS, is it safe?" (No — HTTPS means the connection is encrypted, not that the site is legitimate.) This is a common misconception worth testing directly.
